texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stone's representation theorem asserts a duality between Boolean algebras on the one hand and Stone spaces, which are compact, Hausdorff, and totally disconnected, on the other. This duality implies a natural isomorphism between the homeomorphism group of the space and the automorphism group of the algebra. We introduce a complex of cuts on which these groups act, and prove that when the algebra is countable and the space has at least five points, that these groups are the full automorphism group of the complex.
texts.text: Stone's representation theorem asserts a duality between Boolean algebras on the one hand and Stone spaces, which are compact, Hausdorff, and totally disconnected, on the other. This duality implies a natural isomorphism between the homeomorphism group of the space and the automorphism group of the algebra. We introduce a complex of cuts on which these groups act, and prove that when the algebra is countable and the space has at least five points, that these groups are the full automorphism group of the complex.

texts.orig: A Boolean algebra is a nonempty set B equipped with a pair of binary operations ∨ and ∧ , special elements 0 and 1, and a unary operation ¬ that satisfy simple axioms inspired by the algebra of logical statements (where ∨ and ∧ are 'or' and 'and', 0 is 'false' and 1 is 'true' and ¬ is 'not', respectively) or the algebra of subsets of a set (where 0 is the empty set, 1 is the entire set, and the operations are union, intersection and complementation). Boolean algebras turn out to be partially ordered-in fact, lattices; in the cases where the elements of the algebra are subsets of a set, the partial order is inclusion.

Boolean algebras turn out to be partially ordered-in fact, lattices; in the cases where the elements of the algebra are subsets of a set, the partial order is inclusion.

texts.orig: It turns out that the complement of U b is U ¬ b (because ultrafilters have the maximality property: for each b ∈ B and ultrafilter ω , either b ∈ ω or ¬ b ∈ ω but not both). It is also an easy exercise to verify that this basis of open sets generates a topology which is Hausdorff, since distinct ultrafilters must disagree on some element of B . Compactness requires the Axiom of Choice.

texts.orig: Briefly a cut in E is a partition of E into two disjoint clopen sets U /unionsq V . A cut is peripheral if either U or V has 0 or 1 element. Two cuts U /unionsq V and U ′ /unionsq V ′ cross if all four of the pairwise intersections U ∩ U ′ , U ∩ V ′ , V ∩ U ′ and V ∩ V ′ are nonempty, otherwise they are compatible. The complex of cuts has as vertices the non-peripheral cuts in E , with two cuts being adjacent in C when they are compatible. To think of C as a complex, one may, as is standard with simplicial graphs, add a simplex when its 1-skeleton is present.

texts.orig: Definition 2. A homomorphism of Boolean algebras f : A → B is a map f : A → B of sets which preserves meets and joins, and sends 1 ∈ A to 1 ∈ B and 0 ∈ A to 0 ∈ B . It follows from the axioms that f ( ¬ a ) = ¬ f ( a ) as well. Thus there is a category of Boolean algebras and homomorphisms. The terminal Boolean algebra is 1 = { 0 = 1 } , while the initial one is 2 = { 0 , 1 } .

texts.orig: Lemma 5. Assuming Zorn's Lemma, every filter ω on a Boolean algebra B may be extended to an ultrafilter. A filter is an ultrafilter if and only if, for each b ∈ B , either b ∈ ω or ¬ b ∈ ω , but not both. If f : B → 2 is a homomorphism, the collection of b ∈ B for which f ( b ) = 1 ∈ 2 is an ultrafilter. Finally, suppose that S ⊂ B is a subset with the property that every ultrafilter on B contains an element of S . Then S has a finite subset S 0 with the same property.

A filter is an ultrafilter if and only if, for each b ∈ B , either b ∈ ω or ¬ b ∈ ω , but not both. If f : B → 2 is a homomorphism, the collection of b ∈ B for which f ( b ) = 1 ∈ 2 is an ultrafilter. Finally, suppose that S ⊂ B is a subset with the property that every ultrafilter on B contains an element of S . Then S has a finite subset S 0 with the same property.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Suppose first that ω is a filter. The collection of filters which contain ω as a subset is partially ordered by inclusion. Supposing ω 1 ≤ ω 2 ≤ · · · is a chain of filters containing ω , set ω ∞ = ⋃ ∞ n =1 ω n . This is a proper nonempty subset of B , (since each ω n is a proper subset, none of the ω n contain 0, for instance). If a ∈ ω ∞ and b ∈ ω ∞ , then they are each contained in some ω n , which contains a ∧ b on account of being a filter. Similarly we see that ω ∞ is upward closed, so ω ∞ is a filter which is an upper bound for our given chain. By Zorn's lemma, our poset then has maximal elements, which are ultrafilters by definition.

Since we assume that S = E ( B ) is compact, this always gives Homeo( S ) the structure of a topological group. When S itself has a subbasis of open sets, we may restrict ourselves to choosing U from this subbasis to produce a subbasis for the compact-open topology.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider the case when B is countable, Aut( B ) is non-Archimedean and Polish. Since a Boolean algebra B is, in particular, a poset, we may consider the 1-skeleton of its geometric realization, a simplicial graph we will call Γ B . Vertices of Γ B are thus elements of B , and we have a directed edge from a to b (which we assume distinct) if a ≤ b in the partial order. In terms of the Boolean algebra, this is the case when a ∨ b = b . When B is countable, this is a countable graph and we see immediately that the action of Aut( B ) on the vertex set of Γ B is faithful.

Although this defines a left-invariant metric on Sym( N ), this metric is not complete [Cam96]. Define instead d (Φ , Ψ) = min { δ (Φ , Ψ) , δ (Φ -1 , Ψ -1 ) } . This metric is complete: if Φ n is a Cauchy sequence, one can show that Φ n ( k ) and Φ -1 n ( k ) stabilize for large n , and therefore define a map (which turns out to be a bijection) Φ: N → N by the rule that Φ( j ) = lim n →∞ Φ n ( j ).

texts.orig: Let us return to Boolean algebras. Since the operations of meet and join are recoverable from the poset structure (for example a ∧ b is the greatest lower bound of a and b ), if an element of Aut(Γ B ) preserves directed edges, we claim that actually it comes from an element of Aut( B ). Indeed, since 0 is the minimum and 1 the maximum element of B , we have that an element of Aut(Γ B ) preserving directed edges preserves 0, 1, ∧ and ∨ , hence comes from an automorphism of B . In general (i.e. for Boolean algebras satisfying 0 = 1), an element of Aut(Γ B ) must either fix 0 (and hence fix 1) or swap them, so we have that Aut( B ) is an index-two subgroup of Aut(Γ B ) (in general). In the permutation topology on Aut(Γ B ), we see that Aut( B ) = Stab(0) is open (hence closed by general theory of topological groups).

texts.orig: Since Γ B is simplicial, the edge relation on Γ B is described by a subset of the set of unordered pair of vertices of Γ B . The group Sym( N ) acts continuously on the vertex set of Γ B when given the discrete topology, hence continuously on the set of unordered pairs. An element of Aut(Γ B ) is precisely an element of Sym( N ) that preserves the edge relation. Like all subsets of the (discrete) set of unordered pairs, the edge relation is closed. Since Sym( N ) acts continuously, we see that for each unordered pair [ x, y ], the map Φ ↦→ [Φ( x ) , Φ( y )] is continuous, hence the subgroup of Sym( N ) preserving the edge relation is closed in Sym( N ). Thus we see that Aut( B ) is a closed subgroup of Sym( N ).

texts.orig: As already mentioned, the complex C ( E ) is empty when E has three or fewer points. When E is a four-point set, the complex C ( E ) is a set of three points (with no edges), and the map Homeo( E ) → Aut( C ) is the exceptional map S 4 → S 3 whose kernel is the Klein 4-group generated by the products of disjoint transpositions. When E is a five-point set, the graph C ( E ) is the Petersen graph (cuts are in one-to-one correspondence with two-element subsets of five points, and edges between cuts correspond to disjointness of the two-element subsets), which is well-known to have automorphism group S 5 .

texts.orig: Definition 11. A Stone space system is a properly nested collection of Stone spaces E n ⊆ E n -1 ⊆ · · · ⊆ E 2 ⊆ E 1 for some n ≥ 2. A cut of a Stone space system is a cut of E 1 . A homeomorphism of Stone space systems is a homeomorphism of E 1 which preserves each E k for 2 ≤ k ≤ n . The number n is called the length of the Stone space system.

texts.orig: Lemma 16. The graph C ( E ) is connected if E has at least five points. Moreover if E is finite, then C ( E ) has diameter at most four, while if E is infinite, C ( E ) has diameter two.
texts.text: Lemma 16. The graph C ( E ) is connected if E has at least five points. Moreover if E is finite, then C ( E ) has diameter at most four, while if E is infinite, C ( E ) has diameter two.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Suppose that E is finite but has at least five points. Any choice U γ of two points from E determines a cut U γ /unionsq ( E -U γ ), and any cut γ is compatible with a cut γ ′ , one of whose sets U γ ′ has size two. So beginning with cuts γ and η , replace them with compatible cuts γ ′ and η ′ respectively whose smaller subset U γ ′ and U η ′ has size two. If γ ′ and η ′ are compatible (or equal), then we are done. If not, then U γ ′ ∪ U η ′ has size three, and since E has at least five elements, this determines a cut compatible with both γ ′ and η ′ . This proves that C ( E ) has diameter at most four when E is finite.

However, the corollary that we will derive from it, namely that isomorphisms of complexes of cuts send outermost cuts to outermost cuts, is still true for E having between four and six elements; this is because either every cut is outermost (for four and five) or outermost cuts are distinguished in C ( E ) from others by their valence in C ( E ) (for six).

Each such graph A (Γ) has three vertices, so each vertex of the graph has at most valence two, regardless of whether it is outermost, and there exist nonoutermost cuts in E in this case. However, the corollary that we will derive from it, namely that isomorphisms of complexes of cuts send outermost cuts to outermost cuts, is still true for E having between four and six elements; this is because either every cut is outermost (for four and five) or outermost cuts are distinguished in C ( E ) from others by their valence in C ( E ) (for six).

texts.orig: Proof. Suppose that γ = U /unionsq V is a cut in E which is not outermost. Then since E has at least seven elements, we have that U has at least three and V at least four (up to swapping them). In particular U contains a clopen subset U ′ with at least two elements and V contains disjoint clopen subsets V ′ and V ′′ whose union is V and for which each subset contains at least two elements. These sets yield cuts γ 1 , γ 2 and γ 3 . We claim that γ is adjacent to each of these cuts in some pants decomposition containing all four. Indeed, by definition, we may further divide U ′ into clopen subsets U ′ + and U ′-with at least one element, and one sees directly that U ′ + /unionsq V ′ is half a cut which crosses γ and γ 1 , and that by using the clopen complement of U ′ in U together with halves of V ′ and V ′′ , we can construct cuts that cross γ and γ 2 and cuts that cross γ and γ 3 . By choosing these halves to be sides of cuts in our pants decomposition if necessary, we can show that these cuts cross only those elements, showing that γ is adjacent to γ 1 , γ 2 and γ 3 as desired.

texts.orig: Now suppose that γ = U /unionsq V is outermost with U containing two elements. Since E contains at least seven elements, in any pants decomposition Γ of E containing γ , every cut γ n ∈ Γ has a side V n contained in V . Moreover, we have that the poset of clopen subsets V n properly contained in V has at most two maximal elements with respect to inclusion. Indeed, if it had three, say V 1 , V 2 and V 3 , then V 1 /unionsq V 2 would determine a cut compatible with every cut in Γ, in contradiction to the assumption that Γ is a pants decomposition. Arguing as before, we can show that γ is adjacent to the cuts determined by V 1 and V 2 and no others.

texts.orig: Proof. Indeed, suppose γ = U /unionsq V is adjacent to η = U ′ /unionsq V ′ , where U = { y, z } has size two and γ and η form a peripheral pair. We may suppose that U ′ = U /unionsq { x } . Suppose γ is adjacent to another cut δ = U ′′ /unionsq V ′′ in Γ. Then without loss of generality U ′ ⊂ U ′′ . Both sides of any cut κ witnessing the adjacency of γ and δ must have nonempty intersection with U and with V . The only nonperipheral choices for such a κ force one side of κ to be either { x, y } or { x, z } , since any other choice produces a cut which crosses η . But these choices produce cuts κ which fail to cross δ . This contradiction shows that η is the only cut in Γ to which γ is adjacent.

texts.orig: Observe that if γ = U /unionsq V is a cut in E , we may form two new Stone spaces, namely U /unionsq { V } and V /unionsq { U } by alternately collapsing all points in V to a singleton or collapsing all points in U . Observe that every non-peripheral cut in C ( E ) compatible with γ yields a non-peripheral cut in one of these Stone spaces but not both, and conversely, every non-peripheral cut in one of these Stone spaces yields a cut in E compatible with γ . Indeed, it is not hard to see that L ( γ ) is isomorphic to the join of the graphs C ( U /unionsq { V } ) and C ( V /unionsq { U } ). If γ is not outermost, then both of these graphs are nonempty and we see that L ( γ ) ⊥ has two components.

texts.orig: Here is some intuition. Imagine embedding E into S 2 (this is, of course, possible if and only if E is second countable) and realizing a cut γ as a Jordan curve (also called γ ) in S 2 -E which separates points of U from those of V . The cut γ is peripheral if and only if one of the two components of S 2 -( E ∪ γ ) contains exactly one point of E (i.e. is homeomorphic to an open annulus). Every cut in C ( E ) compatible with yet distinct from γ may be drawn on S 2 disjoint from γ ; thus it is contained in one of the two components of S 2 -( E ∪ γ ). One such component is homeomorphic to the complement in S 2 of the Stone space U /unionsq { V } , while the other is homeomorphic to the complement of V /unionsq { U } . If η is a nonperipheral cut in E compatible with γ , it is a nonperipheral cut in one component of the complement and does not appear in the other.

Since one forms the join Γ /star Γ ′ of two graphs Γ and Γ ′ by beginning with the disjoint union and connecting every vertex of Γ to every vertex of Γ ′ , we have that (Γ /star Γ ′ ) ⊥ = Γ ⊥ /unionsq Γ ′⊥ , which has at least two connected components when Γ and Γ ′ are nonempty. Indeed, in the case of L ( γ ) ⊥ , this graph has exactly two components, since in every Stone space with at least four points, for any two compatible cuts it's easy to see there is a third which crosses both of them.

texts.orig: Proof. From the foregoing discussion, we may think of a cut δ in L ( γ ) as coming from a cut in one of the Stone spaces U /unionsq { V } or V /unionsq { U } , where γ = U /unionsq V . If δ also belongs to L ( η ), then δ also corresponds to a cut in U ′ /unionsq { V ′ } or in V ′ /unionsq { U ′ } . Since γ and η are compatible, we may suppose that U ∩ U ′ = ∅ , or in other words, that there are no cuts in L ( γ ) ∩ L ( η ) corresponding to cuts in both U /unionsq{ V } and U ′ /unionsq{ V ′ } . If γ and η form a peripheral pair, if follows that V ∩ V ′ is a one-point set, and that there is again no cut δ which is distinct from γ and η but which corresponds to a cut in both V /unionsq { U } and V ′ /unionsq { U ′ } , while if γ and η do not form a peripheral pair, there is such a cut δ .

If neither γ nor η is outermost, both U /unionsq{ V } and U ′ /unionsq{ V ′ } admit nonperipheral cuts, so there are at least two components in ( L ( γ ) ∩ L ( η )) ⊥ . Assuming that γ = U /unionsq V , that η = U ′ /unionsq V ′ , and that U ∩ U ′ = ∅ , cuts in the third complementary component of S 2 -( E ∪ γ ∪ η ) correspond to cuts in the Stone space ( V ∩ V ′ ) /unionsq { U, U ′ } . This space has nonperipheral cuts precisely when γ and η do not form a peripheral pair. Therefore ( L ( γ ) ∩ L ( η )) ⊥ has two connected components if and only if the non-outermost cuts γ and η form a peripheral pair.

texts.orig: Perhaps the easiest way to see this is to form the graph dual to this system of Jordan curves on S 2 . That is, place a vertex for each complementary component and connect components via an edge when they border the same curve. This graph is a tree, since S 2 is simply connected and any loop in the dual graph would be homotopically nontrivial in the surface. It is a standard fact that a tree with k edges has k +1 vertices.

texts.orig: Definition 32. A sphere in E with n punctures and k boundary components: is a collection of k pairwise-compatible non-peripheral cuts γ 1 , . . . , γ k in E , thought of as partitions U 1 /unionsq V 1 through U k /unionsq V k such that each U i ∩ U j for i and j distinct is empty and the total intersection V 1 ∩··· ∩ V k is a finite set of size n ≥ 0. The cuts γ 1 , . . . , γ k are said to be the boundary components of the sphere S , and the points of the total intersection are said to be punctures. A cut γ distinct from each of the γ i is said to be in the interior of such a sphere S is whenever a side of γ contains a point of U i , it contains U i completely. Said another way, in this situation, one of the components of ( L ( γ 1 ) ∩ · · · ∩ L ( γ k )) ⊥ corresponds to nonperipheral cuts in the Stone space ( V 1 ∩··· ∩ V k ) /unionsq { U 1 , . . . , U k } ; a cut γ is interior provided it corresponds to a nonperipheral cut in this Stone space.

texts.orig: Definition 33. A principal spherical exhaustion of E is a collection of spheres S 1 , S 2 , . . . satisfying the following conditions.
texts.text: Definition 33. A principal spherical exhaustion of E is a collection of spheres S 1 , S 2 , . . . satisfying the following conditions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. (Increasing) Each boundary component of S i is an interior cut in S i +1 .
texts.text: (Increasing) Each boundary component of S i is an interior cut in S i +1 .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. (Exhaustion) Each cut γ in E is interior to some S n .
texts.text: (Exhaustion) Each cut γ in E is interior to some S n .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. (Complexity) Each sphere formed from a boundary component of S i together with those from S i +1 , supposing it has n punctures and k boundary components, satisfies n + k ≥ 5.
texts.text: (Complexity) Each sphere formed from a boundary component of S i together with those from S i +1 , supposing it has n punctures and k boundary components, satisfies n + k ≥ 5.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. (Infinite Complement) For each boundary component γ = U /unionsq V of S n where U is chosen in the notation as above, the clopen set U is infinite.
texts.text: (Infinite Complement) For each boundary component γ = U /unionsq V of S n where U is chosen in the notation as above, the clopen set U is infinite.

texts.orig: By beginning with a pants decomposition of E and forgetting certain pants curves, one sees that principal spherical exhaustions exist whenever E is infinite and second countable. For each sphere S i in the exhaustion, let ¯ S i be a finite set with n + k points, one for each puncture and boundary component of S i . Then observe that there exist continuous maps E → ¯ S i and ¯ S i +1 → ¯ S i : for the former, one collapses each clopen set U for each boundary component in S i to a single point; continuity follows because U is clopen. If ( x i ) is such a sequence, we may think of it in E as a choice, for each i , of a puncture or boundary component of S i with the compatibility property that the puncture or boundary component chosen at the ( i +1)st stage is contained in the boundary component chosen at the i th stage (or equal to if the i th choice is a puncture). This yields a chain of nonempty clopen subsets of E , say U 1 ⊃ U 2 ⊃ · · · . Any point in the total intersection of these sets will be mapped by Φ to the sequence ( x i ), so the map Φ is surjective. By the (Exhaustion) property, if x = y in E , every curve γ which separates x from y is interior to some S i , and thus the Φ-images of x and y are distinct.

texts.orig: Lemma 35. Suppose that E is infinite and second countable. If Φ: C ( E ) → C ( E ′ ) is an isomorphism of complexes of cuts and S 1 , S 2 , . . . is a principal spherical exhaustion of E , the cuts comprising each sphere S i assemble into a sphere in E ′ with the same number of punctures and boundary components. Moreover, these spheres are a principal spherical exhaustion Φ( S 1 ) , Φ( S 2 ) , . . . of E ′ , which is also infinite and second countable.

texts.orig: Consider the collection of cuts formed by the boundary components of S i +1 and S i , and let A denote the adjacency graph of these cuts. By Lemma 30, the subgraph comprising the boundary components of S i is complete, as is each of the subgraphs comprising one of the spheres S ′ as above. Since by (Complexity) each boundary component of S i divides S i +1 into two spheres, the corresponding vertex of A is a cut vertex-removing it disconnects the graph into two components. In fact, one may argue directly that after replacing each of these vertices with two vertices, the graph A is a disjoint union of k +1 complete graphs, where k is the number of boundary components of S i . Similarly one may argue that L ⊥ , the opposite graph of the intersection of links, has k +1 finite components, one corresponding to S i , and k corresponding to the boundary components of S i . Each component of the cut vertex decomposition of A corresponds uniquely to a finite component of L ⊥ .

texts.orig: 4 Faithfulness of the Action
texts.text: 4 Faithfulness of the Action

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To complete the proof of Theorem 10, we need to show that Homeo( E ) acts faithfully on C ( E ) when E has at least five elements. This, together with the foregoing theorem, shows that the map Homeo( E ) → Aut( C ) is a continuous bijective homomorphism. To show that it is an isomorphism of topological groups, we need to know that the map is open.

texts.text: Lemma 36. Suppose that E is second countable and infinite. If K is compact and U is clopen such that K ⊂ U , there exists a finite subset G ⊂ C such that V ( K,U ) in Homeo( E ) contains Stab( G ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Since K is compact, we may cover it with finitely many disjoint clopen sets U 1 , . . . , U n . We may assume that these sets lie within U . Each of the sets U 1 , . . . , U n and ⋃ i U i (which we may assume without loss by shrinking U is actually U ) is half of a cut in E , but not all of these cuts are non-peripheral. Considering only those cuts which are non-peripheral, we see we have a sphere S in E . When the complexity of this sphere is at least 5, we claim that if an element f of Homeo( E ) fixes each boundary cut of S and each interior cut of S , then f ∈ V ( K,U ). (Indeed, some smaller finite number of cuts should suffice, but we have no need for optimality.)
texts.text: Proof. Since K is compact, we may cover it with finitely many disjoint clopen sets U 1 , . . . , U n . We may assume that these sets lie within U . Each of the sets U 1 , . . . , U n and ⋃ i U i (which we may assume without loss by shrinking U is actually U ) is half of a cut in E , but not all of these cuts are non-peripheral. Considering only those cuts which are non-peripheral, we see we have a sphere S in E . When the complexity of this sphere is at least 5, we claim that if an element f of Homeo( E ) fixes each boundary cut of S and each interior cut of S , then f ∈ V ( K,U ). (Indeed, some smaller finite number of cuts should suffice, but we have no need for optimality.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To see this, note that if f fi xes each boundary cut of S , it clearly descends to an automorphism of the interior cuts in S , hence a permutation of the set of punctures and boundary components of S . Since the total number of these is at least five, we have by the faithfulness of the action that the permutation induced by f is trivial. It follows that f ∈ V ( K,U ), for f must send each U i , which corresponds either to a puncture or a boundary component of S , to itself, hence clearly inside U .
texts.text: To see this, note that if f fi xes each boundary cut of S , it clearly descends to an automorphism of the interior cuts in S , hence a permutation of the set of punctures and boundary components of S . Since the total number of these is at least five, we have by the faithfulness of the action that the permutation induced by f is trivial. It follows that f ∈ V ( K,U ), for f must send each U i , which corresponds either to a puncture or a boundary component of S , to itself, hence clearly inside U .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Supposing that S has smaller complexity, we need to add additional cuts in order to guarantee that f ∈ V ( K,U ). If K is infinite, we may simply choose a finer collection of disjoint clopen sets U i in order to make the complexity of the sphere grow. If K (and without loss of generality U ) is finite, add for each point of K a peripheral pair. Any element of Homeo( E ) fixing the peripheral pair, we have seen, fixes the point. Repeating for each point of K , we conclude.
